---
title: "Auto-evaluation"
page-layout: article
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

```{julia}
#| echo: false
include("activate.jl")
```

We have included in this page a series of **quizzes** to help reinforce your learning. These quizzes are designed not only to test your understanding but also to provide you with an opportunity for **self-reflection**. After each quiz, take a moment to review your answers, reflect on what you've learned, and identify areas where you may need further clarification. Do not hesitate to reach out to your lecturer if any questions come up.

### Core Syntax

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION = 0
nothing #| hide
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Which of the following defines a variable `x` with value `10` in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`let x = 10`",
           "`x := 10`",
           "`x = 10`",
           "`int x = 10`"]
answer = 3
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Which keyword is used to define a function in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`func`",
           "`function`",
           "`def`",
           "`lambda`"]
answer = 2
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Which symbol is used for element-wise operations in Julia (e.g., squaring each element of a vector)?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`:`",
           "`.`",
           "`->`",
           "`*`"]
answer = 2
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of this code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a
b[1] = 10
println(a)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`[10, 2, 3]`",
           "`[1, 2, 3]`",
           "`[1, 10, 3]`",
           "Error."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does the `end` keyword do in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["Ends a program.",
           "Closes a block of code like `if`, `for`, or `function`.",
           "Skips to the next iteration.",
           "Marks a breakpoint."]
answer = 2
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Which expression returns the number of elements in a vector `v`?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`count(v)`",
           "`size(v)`",
           "`dim(v)`",
           "`length(v)`"]
answer = 4
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does `===` do in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["Checks if two expressions are numerically equal.",
           "Checks if two expressions refer to the exact same object in memory.",
           "Compares string equality.",
           "Performs type conversion."]
answer = 2
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
result = 0
for i in 1:10
    if i == 5
        break
    end
    result += i
end
println(result)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`10`",
           "`15`",
           "`55`",
           "`45`"]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
result = Int[]
for i in 1:6
    if i % 2 == 0
        continue
    end
    push!(result, i)
end
println(result)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`[1, 3, 5]`",
           "`[2, 4, 6]`",
           "`[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]`",
           "`[]`"]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Which of the following is a valid `for` loop in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`for i = 1 to 10`",
           "`for i in 1:10`",
           "`foreach i in 1:10`",
           "`for (i = 1; i <= 10; i++)`"]
answer = 2
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [1, 2, 3]
println(a == b)
println(a === b)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`true`, `false` — `==` compares values, `===` checks same object in memory.",
           "`true`, `true` — both operators check value equality.",
           "`false`, `false` — arrays are never considered equal.",
           "`true`, `true` — identical contents imply identical memory."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
x = 7
result = x > 5 ? "big" : "small"
println(result)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`\"big\"`",
           "`\"small\"`",
           "`true`",
           "`7`"]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
v = [1, 2, 3]
println(v .^ 2)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`[1, 4, 9]`",
           "`[2, 4, 6]`",
           "`[1, 2, 3]`",
           "`9` — the last element squared."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

### Type Hierarchies

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION = 0
nothing #| hide
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the purpose of an abstract type in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It defines a concrete implementation for other types.",
           "It can be instantiated and used directly.",
           "It provides a blueprint for organizing related types but cannot be instantiated.",
           "It is used for type annotations in functions."]
answer = 3
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Which of the following types is a concrete type?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["AbstractFloat", "Real", "Int64", "Number"]
answer = 3
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does the `isconcretetype` function return for `AbstractFloat`?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["true", "false", "Error: Undefined type", "null"]
answer = 2
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"""
**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What will the following code return?**
"""
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
typeof(42)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["Int64", "Integer", "Number", "Real"]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the purpose of the `isa` operator in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["To check if a variable's value matches a specific type.",
           "To check if a variable is an instance of a specific type or any of its subtypes.",
           "To check if a type is concrete.",
           "To check if a variable is a subtype of `Any`."]
answer = 2
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"""
**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What will be the result of the following code?**
"""
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
Int64 <: Real
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["true", "false", "Error: Type mismatch", "true for Float64 but not for Int64"]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"""
**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What will the following code return?**
"""
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
isconcretetype(Int64)
isconcretetype(AbstractFloat)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["true for Int64 and false for AbstractFloat",
           "false for both types",
           "true for AbstractFloat and false for Int64",
           "false for both types if using a different syntax"]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"""
**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does the `<:` operator check in Julia?**
"""
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["If one type is a subtype of another.",
           "If two types are exactly the same.",
           "If a type can be instantiated.",
           "If a type is abstract."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the result of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
subtypes(Real)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["Returns a list of all types that are subtypes of `Real`.",
           "Returns an error because `Real` is abstract.",
           "Returns `Any` as the only subtype of `Real`.",
           "Shows `Real` as a parent type with no subtypes."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"""
**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does the `supertype` function return for `Float64`?**
"""
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
supertype(Float64)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["Int", "Real", "Number", "AbstractFloat"]
answer = 4
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
println(42 isa Int64)
println(42 isa Real)
println(Int64 <: Real)
println(42 <: Real)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`true`, `true`, `true`, `false` (or error) — `<:` is for types, not values.",
           "`true`, `true`, `true`, `true` — all four return `true`.",
           "`true`, `false`, `true`, `false` — `isa Real` only matches abstract types.",
           "`false`, `false`, `true`, `false` — `isa` only matches concrete types."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Starting from `Float64`, what is the correct chain of supertypes up to `Any`?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`Float64` → `AbstractFloat` → `Real` → `Number` → `Any`",
           "`Float64` → `Real` → `Number` → `Any`",
           "`Float64` → `Number` → `Real` → `AbstractFloat` → `Any`",
           "`Float64` → `AbstractFloat` → `Number` → `Any`"]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

### Type Conversion and Promotion


```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION = 0
nothing #| hide
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does the `convert` function do in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It converts a value from one type to another, if possible.",
           "It automatically promotes values to a common type.",
           "It converts numbers to strings.",
           "It changes a value to a boolean type."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
println(round(Int, 3.14))
println(floor(Int, 3.14))
println(convert(Float64, 5))
println(string(123))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["3, 3, 5.0, \"123\"",
           "3, 3, 5.0, 123",
           "3, 3.14, 5, \"123\"",
           "5.0, 3, 5, \"123\""]
answer = 2
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What happens when an integer is assigned to a `Float64` variable in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
y::Float64 = 10
println(y)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["Julia automatically converts the integer to a Float64.",
           "Julia throws a type error because of the type mismatch.",
           "The conversion needs to be done explicitly using `convert`.",
           "The variable `y` will be set to the integer value of 10."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does the `promote` function do in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
a, b = promote(3, 4.5)
println(a)
println(b)
println(typeof(a))
println(typeof(b))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It promotes two values to a common type for an operation.",
           "It converts both values to integers.",
           "It converts values to strings for display.",
           "It checks if two values have the same type."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What will happen if we try to add an `Int` and a `String` in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
println(3 + "Hello")
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It will throw a type error.",
           "It will concatenate the string and the number.",
           "It will promote the number to a string.",
           "Julia will automatically convert both to a common type."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What are the types of `a`, `b`, and `c` after the following `promote` call?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
a, b, c = promote(1, 2.0, 3//1)
println(typeof(a), " ", typeof(b), " ", typeof(c))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`Float64 Float64 Float64` — all promoted to the common type.",
           "`Int64 Float64 Rational{Int64}` — no promotion occurs.",
           "`Int64 Int64 Int64` — floats are converted to Int.",
           "`Rational Float64 Rational` — Rational is the common type."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
println(floor(Int, -2.3))
println(round(Int, -2.5))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`-3`, `-2` — `floor` rounds toward −∞; `round` uses banker's rounding.",
           "`-2`, `-3` — both round toward zero.",
           "`-3`, `-3` — both round toward −∞.",
           "`-2`, `-2` — both round toward zero."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

### Special Types

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION = 0
nothing #| hide
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does the `Nothing` type represent in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It represents the absence of a meaningful value.",
           "It is used for undefined variables.",
           "It is a placeholder for missing data.",
           "It is a special type for numeric values."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the result of calling the following function in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
# Example of a function that returns `Nothing`
function print_message(msg::String)
    println(msg)
    return nothing  # Explicitly returns `nothing`
end

result = print_message("Hello!")
println(result === nothing)  # Output: true
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`nothing` is returned and the output is true.",
           "`nothing` is returned but the output is false.",
           "The function throws an error because nothing cannot be returned.",
           "The function returns a string 'nothing'."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What happens to Julia's compiler when a function's return type is inferred as `Any`?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["The compiler cannot optimize the function, which degrades performance.",
           "The function runs faster because it skips all type checks.",
           "The function becomes type-stable by definition.",
           "The function can only accept `Any`-typed arguments."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does the following code do in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
data = [1, 2, missing, 4, 5]
for item in data
    if item === missing
        println("Missing data detected.")
    else
        println("Value: ", item)
    end
end
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It checks for missing values and prints a message for each.",
           "It sums all the values and skips missing ones.",
           "It throws an error when encountering missing data.",
           "It replaces missing data with a default value."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the purpose of the `skipmissing` function in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
using Statistics

# Example array with missing values
data = [1, 2, missing, 4, 5, missing, 7]

# Summing values while skipping missing entries
sum_no_missing = sum(skipmissing(data))
println("Sum without missing values: ", sum_no_missing)  # Output: 19

# Calculating the mean while skipping missing values
mean_no_missing = mean(skipmissing(data))
println("Mean without missing values: ", mean_no_missing)  # Output: 3.8
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It creates an iterator that skips `missing` values during computations.",
           "It replaces `missing` values with `0`.",
           "It prints out the number of `missing` values.",
           "It raises an error if `missing` values are encountered."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
data = Union{Int, Missing}[1, missing, 3]
println(typeof(data[2]))
println(ismissing(data[2]))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`Missing`, `true`",
           "`Nothing`, `false`",
           "`Missing`, `false`",
           "`Int64`, `true`"]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function find_value(d::Dict, key)
    return get(d, key, nothing)
end

result = find_value(Dict("a" => 1), "b")
println(result === nothing)
println(typeof(result))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`true`, `Nothing` — the key does not exist; `get` returns the default.",
           "`false`, `Int64` — the key is found.",
           "`true`, `Missing` — missing and nothing are the same.",
           "`false`, `Nothing` — the function always returns `Nothing`."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

### Union Types

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION = 0
nothing #| hide
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is a `Union` type in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["A type that allows a variable to accept multiple types.",
           "A type that restricts a variable to only one type.",
           "A built-in function for type conversion.",
           "A type that can only accept floating-point numbers."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function process_number(x::Union{Int, Float64})
    println("The input is: ", x)
end

process_number(5)       # Works with an Int
process_number(3.14)    # Works with a Float64
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["The input is: 5, The input is: 3.14",
           "The input is: 5, The input is: 3",
           "The input is: 3.14, The input is: 5",
           "The input is: 5, The input is: 3.0"]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Which of the following scenarios would benefit from using a `Union` type?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
# Example using Union to handle multiple types in a function
function add_one(x::Union{Int, Float64})
    return x + 1
end

println(add_one(3))     # Output: 4 (Int)
println(add_one(2.5))   # Output: 3.5 (Float64)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["When a function needs to accept both integers and floating-point numbers.",
           "When a function accepts only integers.",
           "When a function is only designed to accept floating-point numbers.",
           "When there is a strict requirement to accept a specific type."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Two methods exist for `process`. Which one is called by `process(3//1)`?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function process(x::Union{Int, Float64})
    return x * 2
end

function process(x::Real)
    return x + 1
end

println(process(3//1))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`6//1` — the `Union{Int, Float64}` method is called because `Rational <: Int`.",
           "`4//1` — the `Real` method is called because `Rational` is not `Int` or `Float64`.",
           "`6.0` — the value is promoted to `Float64`.",
           "`MethodError` — no matching method exists."]
answer = 2
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function add_one(x::Union{Int, Float64})
    return x + 1
end

println(add_one(Int8(3)))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`4` — `Int8` is a subtype of `Int`, so the method matches.",
           "`4` as `Int8`.",
           "`MethodError` — `Int8` is not `Int` (which is `Int64` on 64-bit systems).",
           "`4.0` — `Int8` is promoted to `Float64`."]
answer = 3
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does `Int <: Union{Int, Float64}` return, and what does it mean?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`true` — `Int` is one of the types listed in the `Union`, so it is a subtype.",
           "`false` — `Int` is a concrete type, so it cannot be a subtype of a `Union`.",
           "`true` — every type is a subtype of every `Union`.",
           "`false` — `<:` only works between two concrete types."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

### Type Annotations and Declarations

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION = 0
nothing #| hide
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the primary purpose of type annotations in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["To make code run faster by skipping type checks.",
           "To specify the exact memory address of a variable.",
           "To provide clarity in the code and enable optimizations by the compiler.",
           "To prevent errors from occurring in the code."]
answer = 3
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Which of the following correctly applies a type annotation to a variable?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["a = 10::Int",
           "Int::a = 10",
           "a::Int = 10",
           "a:Int = 10"]
answer = 3
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"""
**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What will happen if the following code is executed?**
"""
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function add(a::Int, b::Int)
    return a + b
end

add(3, "4")
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It will throw a type error because `\"4\"` is a String, not an Int.",
           "It will convert \"4\" to an Int and return 7.",
           "It will throw a syntax error.",
           "It will ignore the type annotation and return 7."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). In Julia, what will the following code output?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function multiply(a::Int, b::Int)::Int
    return a * b
end
multiply(3, 4)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["12", "12.0", "Error: Incorrect type", "Nothing"]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What happens when the following function is called with `f(\"hello\")`?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function f(x::T) where T <: Real
    return x * 2
end

f("hello")
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`MethodError` — `String` is not a subtype of `Real`.",
           "`\"hellohello\"` — the `*` operator works on strings too.",
           "`TypeError` — the return type annotation is violated.",
           "The function works because `T` is inferred as `String`."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

### Parametric Types

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION = 0
nothing #| hide
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
struct MyPair{T, S}
    first::T
    second::S
end

p = MyPair(1, 2.0)
println(typeof(p))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`MyPair{Int64, Float64}`",
           "`MyPair{Number, Number}`",
           "`MyPair{Any, Any}`",
           "`MyPair{Int64, Int64}`"]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the role of `T` and `S` in the `Pair` struct example?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
struct Pair{T, S}
    first::T
    second::S
end

pair1 = Pair(1, "apple")
pair2 = Pair(3.14, true)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`T` and `S` define the data types of the first and second elements of the pair.",
           "`T` is used for the first element, and `S` is used for the second element.",
           "`T` and `S` are unused in this case, they are placeholders.",
           "`T` defines the data type of both elements in the pair."]
answer = 2
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What happens when you instantiate `Pair(1, 'apple')` in the provided code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
pair1 = Pair(1, "apple")
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It will create a pair with an `Int` and a `String`.",
           "It will throw an error because `Int` and `String` can't be combined.",
           "It will create a pair with `Int` and `String`.",
           "It will cause a runtime error because the types don't match."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the benefit of using parametric types like `AbstractContainer{T}`?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
abstract type AbstractContainer{T} end

struct VectorContainer{T} <: AbstractContainer{T}
    data::Vector{T}
end

struct SetContainer{T} <: AbstractContainer{T}
    data::Set{T}
end
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It allows you to specify concrete types directly in the struct.",
           "It makes the code less flexible and more specific.",
           "It allows you to create types that can handle any type of data, with type safety.",
           "It makes the code more complex and harder to maintain."]
answer = 3
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does the `print_container_info` function do?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function print_container_info(container::AbstractContainer{T}) where T
    println("Container holds values of type: ", T)
end
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It prints the type of the values in the container.",
           "It prints the number of elements in the container.",
           "It prints the values inside the container.",
           "It returns the type of the container."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the purpose of `AbstractContainer{T}` in the code example?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
abstract type AbstractContainer{T} end
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It defines a concrete container type.",
           "It defines an abstract type that can be used to create containers for any data type `T`.",
           "It defines a container for a specific type of data.",
           "It restricts containers to hold only numeric types."]
answer = 2
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What would be the output of `print_container_info(vec)` if `vec` is `VectorContainer([1, 2, 3])`?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
vec = VectorContainer([1, 2, 3])
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It will print `Container holds values of type: Int64`.",
           "It will print `Container holds values of type: Vector{Int64}`.",
           "It will print the values inside the container.",
           "It will throw an error because `VectorContainer` is not defined."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). How does using parametric types help with code reusability?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It reduces the need to define separate functions for different data types.",
           "It forces you to create new types for every use case.",
           "It makes the code less reusable.",
           "It requires more boilerplate code."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
struct RealPair{T <: Real}
    first::T
    second::T
end

println(typeof(RealPair(1, 2)))
println(typeof(RealPair(1.0, 2.0)))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`RealPair{Int64}`, `RealPair{Float64}`",
           "`RealPair{Real}`, `RealPair{Real}`",
           "`RealPair{Number}`, `RealPair{Number}`",
           "`RealPair{Any}`, `RealPair{Any}`"]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What happens when `RealPair(\"a\", \"b\")` is called?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`TypeError` — `String` violates the `T <: Real` constraint.",
           "`RealPair{String}` is created because `String` is concrete.",
           "`RealPair{Any}` is created as a fallback.",
           "The constructor silently converts the strings to numbers."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

### Errors and Exception Handling

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION = 0
nothing #| hide
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Which error type is raised when an index is out of bounds in an array?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["ArgumentError", 
           "BoundsError", 
           "DivisionByZeroError", 
           "IOError"]
answer = 2
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does the following code do in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function divide(a, b)
    if b == 0
        throw(DivideError())
    end
    return a / b
end
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It raises a DivideError when b equals 0.",
           "It raises an ArgumentError when a or b is invalid.",
           "It throws a BoundsError if a or b are not numbers.",
           "It performs division and returns the result."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What happens when the following `try`/`catch` block is executed?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
try
    println(divide(10, 0))  # Will raise an error
catch e
    println("Error: ", e)  # Handles the error
end
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["The program throws an error and stops execution.",
           "The error is caught and a custom error message is printed.",
           "The program silently ignores the error.",
           "The program prints the result of the division."]
answer = 2
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the purpose of the `finally` block in Julia's exception handling?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["To ensure that cleanup code runs regardless of whether an error occurs.",
           "To catch all errors and handle them.",
           "To rethrow any errors that are caught.",
           "To perform the main logic of the program."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function safe_file_read(filename::String)
    file = nothing
    try
        file = open(filename, "r")
        data = read(file, String)
        return data
    catch e
        println("An error occurred: ", e)
    finally
        if file !== nothing
            close(file)
            println("File closed.")
        end
    end
end

# Test with a valid file
println(safe_file_read("example.txt"))

# Test with an invalid file
println(safe_file_read("nonexistent.txt"))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["The program prints data from the file and closes it.",
           "The program raises an error and does not close the file.",
           "The program tries to read a file, catches errors, and always closes the file.",
           "The program prints the error but skips file closing."]
answer = 3
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Which of the following is an appropriate use case for the `finally` block?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["Ensuring a file is closed after reading, regardless of errors.",
           "To catch all exceptions without handling them.",
           "To prevent specific types of errors from being raised.",
           "To handle errors and return a value from the `finally` block."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What happens when `try` is used with `finally` but without `catch`?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
try
    error("oops")
finally
    println("cleanup")
end
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`cleanup` is printed and then the error propagates normally.",
           "The error is silently ignored because `finally` handled it.",
           "`cleanup` is printed and then execution continues normally.",
           "The `finally` block is skipped because there is no `catch`."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What type of error is thrown by `sqrt(-1.0)` in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`DomainError` — the argument is outside the valid domain.",
           "`ArgumentError` — the argument type is incorrect.",
           "`MethodError` — no `sqrt` method exists for negative numbers.",
           "`InexactError` — the result cannot be represented exactly."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

### Performance

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION = 0
nothing #| hide
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does it mean for a function in Julia to be *type stable*?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It always returns an Int64.", 
           "The return type can be predicted at compile time from the input types.", 
           "It never allocates memory.", 
           "It always avoids recursion."]
answer = 2
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Which Julia macro is commonly used to check for type stability?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`@time`", 
           "`@inbounds`", 
           "`@code_warntype`", 
           "`@show`"]
answer = 3
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Which of the following best describes a type-unstable function?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["A function where the compiler cannot infer a concrete return type.", 
           "A function that allocates memory unnecessarily.", 
           "A function that returns multiple values.", 
           "A function that runs slower than expected."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Consider the function:**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function g(x)
    return x > 10 ? 10 : "small"
end
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["The function is recursive.", 
           "The function is type stable.", 
           "The function is type unstable.", 
           "The function will always error."]
answer = 3
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). In Julia, which type causes the biggest problems for type inference when used as a return type?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`Int64`", 
           "`Any`", 
           "`Float64`", 
           "`Bool`"]
answer = 2
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Which of the following is volatile (non permanent) memory?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["Hard disk.", 
           "SSD.", 
           "RAM.", 
           "Flash drive."]
answer = 3
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Which component temporarily stores instructions and data for quick access by the CPU?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["Cache.", 
           "ROM.", 
           "Hard disk.", 
           "GPU."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```


```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). The basic unit of data in computer hardware is:**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["Word.", 
           "Byte.", 
           "Bit.", 
           "Nibble."]
answer = 3
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Consider two functions below. Which one is type-stable when called with an `Int` argument?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function f1(x)
    return x > 0 ? x : 0.0
end

function f2(x)
    return x > 0 ? x : zero(x)
end
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`f2` — `zero(x)` preserves the type of `x`, so the return type is always the same.",
           "`f1` — returning `0.0` ensures a consistent `Float64` output.",
           "Both are type-stable since they return a number.",
           "Neither is type-stable because they use a conditional."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). A function returns `Union{Int, Float64}` depending on its input value. What is the main performance consequence?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["The compiler cannot specialize the caller's code, causing dynamic dispatch overhead.",
           "The function runs twice as fast because it handles two types.",
           "There is no consequence; Julia handles `Union` types efficiently.",
           "The function is automatically rewritten by the compiler to return `Float64`."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

### Functions

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION = 0
nothing #| hide
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Which of the following is the correct syntax for a keyword argument with a default value?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`function f(x; n=10)`",
           "`function f(x, n=10)`",
           "`function f(x, n::default=10)`",
           "`function f(x; n::required)`"]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function greet(name; greeting="Hello")
    println("$greeting, $name!")
end

greet("Alice")
greet("Bob", greeting="Hi")
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`Hello, Alice!` then `Hi, Bob!`",
           "`Hello, Alice!` then `Hello, Bob!`",
           "`Hi, Alice!` then `Hi, Bob!`",
           "Error — keyword arguments cannot have default values."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). By convention, what does a trailing `!` in a Julia function name indicate?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["The function modifies (mutates) one or more of its arguments.",
           "The function may throw an exception.",
           "The function is defined in a module.",
           "The function returns a boolean value."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function sum_all(args...)
    total = 0
    for x in args
        total += x
    end
    return total
end

println(sum_all(1, 2, 3))
println(sum_all(10))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`6` then `10`",
           "`6` then `0`",
           "Error — variadic functions cannot have zero extra arguments.",
           "`1` then `10`"]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function double(x)
    x * 2
end

println(double(5))
println(double("hi"))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`10` then `\"hihi\"` — the last expression is the implicit return value.",
           "`10` then error — `*` is not defined for strings.",
           "`nothing` then `nothing` — no explicit `return` means `nothing` is returned.",
           "`10` then `\"hi\"` — strings ignore the multiplier."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
v = [3, 1, 2]
sort(v)
println(v)
sort!(v)
println(v)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`[3, 1, 2]` then `[1, 2, 3]` — `sort` returns a new array; `sort!` mutates in place.",
           "`[1, 2, 3]` then `[1, 2, 3]` — both sort in place.",
           "`[3, 1, 2]` then `[3, 1, 2]` — neither modifies the original.",
           "Error — `sort!` requires a different argument type."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
f(x) = x^2
v = [1, 2, 3, 4]
println(f.(v))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`[1, 4, 9, 16]` — broadcasting applies `f` element-wise.",
           "`[1, 2, 3, 4]` — broadcasting does not change the values.",
           "Error — broadcasting syntax `.` requires the function to accept arrays.",
           "`16` — the function is applied to the entire array."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). Three methods are defined for `describe`. Which method is called by `describe(3.14)`?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
describe(x::Int)     = println("integer: ", x)
describe(x::Float64) = println("float: ", x)
describe(x)          = println("other: ", x)

describe(3.14)
describe(42)
describe("hello")
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`float: 3.14`, `integer: 42`, `other: hello` — Julia dispatches on the most specific matching method.",
           "`other: 3.14`, `other: 42`, `other: hello` — the fallback method is always called.",
           "`float: 3.14`, `float: 42`, `other: hello` — all numbers use the float method.",
           "Error — multiple methods with the same name are not allowed."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

### Data Structures

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION = 0
nothing #| hide
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). How do you create a `Dict` mapping `\"a\"` to `1` and `\"b\"` to `2` in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`Dict(\"a\" => 1, \"b\" => 2)`",
           "`{\"a\": 1, \"b\": 2}`",
           "`Dict[\"a\" = 1, \"b\" = 2]`",
           "`HashMap(\"a\", 1, \"b\", 2)`"]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
d = Dict("x" => 10, "y" => 20)
d["z"] = 30
println(length(d))
println(get(d, "w", -1))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`3` then `-1` — the dict has 3 entries; `get` returns the default for missing keys.",
           "`2` then `-1` — `d[\"z\"] = 30` fails because the key doesn't exist.",
           "`3` then `0` — `get` returns `0` for missing numeric keys.",
           "`3` then error — accessing a missing key throws a `KeyError`."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the difference between `v[2:4]` and `@view v[2:4]`?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`v[2:4]` creates a copy of the slice; `@view v[2:4]` creates a view that shares memory with `v`.",
           "Both create a copy; `@view` is just syntax sugar.",
           "`v[2:4]` creates a view; `@view v[2:4]` creates a copy.",
           "`@view` is only valid for matrices, not vectors."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
A = [1 2; 3 4]
B = [5 6; 7 8]
println(A * B)
println(A .* B)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`[19 22; 43 50]` then `[5 12; 21 32]` — `*` is matrix multiplication; `.*` is element-wise.",
           "`[5 12; 21 32]` then `[19 22; 43 50]` — the operators are swapped.",
           "`[19 22; 43 50]` then `[19 22; 43 50]` — both operators perform matrix multiplication.",
           "Error — matrix operations require the `LinearAlgebra` package."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does the `\\` operator do when applied to a matrix `A` and vector `b`?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It solves the linear system `Ax = b` for `x`.",
           "It performs element-wise division of `A` by `b`.",
           "It computes the matrix inverse of `A`.",
           "It returns the dot product of `A` and `b`."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
A = [2.0 1.0; 1.0 3.0]
b = [5.0, 10.0]
x = A \ b
println(round.(x, digits=4))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`[1.0, 3.0]` — the solution to the 2×2 linear system.",
           "`[2.5, 3.33]` — the element-wise division result.",
           "`[5.0, 10.0]` — the right-hand side is returned unchanged.",
           "Error — `\\` requires an integer matrix."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
d = Dict("score" => 0)

function update!(d, key, val)
    d[key] = val
end

update!(d, "score", 42)
println(d["score"])
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`42` — `Dict` is passed by reference; the function mutates the original.",
           "`0` — the function receives a copy of the dict.",
           "Error — you cannot pass a `Dict` to a function.",
           "`0` — only the `!` convention guarantees in-place mutation."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
v = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
w = v[2:4]
w[1] = 999
println(v[2])
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`20` — `v[2:4]` creates a copy; modifying `w` does not affect `v`.",
           "`999` — slicing creates a view; modifying `w` also modifies `v`.",
           "Error — you cannot assign to a slice.",
           "`30` — Julia shifts the index by one."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

### Composite Types

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION = 0
nothing #| hide
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the key difference between `struct` and `mutable struct` in Julia?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["Fields of a `struct` cannot be changed after creation; fields of a `mutable struct` can.",
           "`struct` allows inheritance; `mutable struct` does not.",
           "`mutable struct` is faster because it avoids type checks.",
           "Both are identical; `mutable` is just a naming convention."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
struct Point
    x::Float64
    y::Float64
end

p = Point(3.0, 4.0)
println(p.x)
println(fieldnames(Point))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`3.0` then `(:x, :y)` — field access uses `.`; `fieldnames` returns a tuple of symbols.",
           "`3.0` then `[\"x\", \"y\"]` — `fieldnames` returns a vector of strings.",
           "`3.0` then `(x, y)` — `fieldnames` returns a tuple of variable names.",
           "Error — `fieldnames` is not defined for user-defined structs."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What happens when you try to modify a field of an immutable `struct`?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
struct Circle
    radius::Float64
end

c = Circle(5.0)
c.radius = 10.0
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["An error is thrown — fields of immutable structs cannot be reassigned.",
           "The field is updated silently.",
           "A new `Circle` is created with the updated radius.",
           "A warning is shown but execution continues."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
mutable struct Counter
    count::Int
end

c = Counter(0)
c.count += 1
c.count += 1
println(c.count)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`2` — `mutable struct` allows field modification.",
           "`0` — the struct is copied on assignment.",
           "Error — `+=` is not defined for struct fields.",
           "`1` — only the last assignment is kept."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
struct LinearTransform
    slope::Float64
    intercept::Float64
end

(lt::LinearTransform)(x) = lt.slope * x + lt.intercept

lt = LinearTransform(2.0, 3.0)
println(lt(5))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`13.0` — the struct is called like a function: `2.0 * 5 + 3.0`.",
           "`10.0` — only the slope is applied.",
           "Error — structs cannot be used as functions.",
           "`13` as `Int` — integer inputs produce integer outputs."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). An inner constructor is defined below. What happens when `SafeValue(-5)` is called?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
struct SafeValue
    value::Float64
    function SafeValue(v)
        v < 0 && error("Value must be non-negative")
        new(v)
    end
end

SafeValue(-5)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["An error is thrown: `\"Value must be non-negative\"`.",
           "`SafeValue(-5.0)` is created silently.",
           "`SafeValue(0.0)` is created — negative values are clamped to 0.",
           "A warning is displayed but the struct is created."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does `fieldnames(Point)` return for `struct Point; x::Float64; y::Float64; end`?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`(:x, :y)` — a tuple of symbols.",
           "`[\"x\", \"y\"]` — a vector of strings.",
           "`(Float64, Float64)` — a tuple of field types.",
           "`2` — the number of fields."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
struct Box{T}
    value::T
end

b1 = Box(42)
b2 = Box(3.14)
println(typeof(b1))
println(typeof(b2))
println(Box{Int} <: Box{Real})
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`Box{Int64}`, `Box{Float64}`, `false` — parametric structs are invariant; `Box{Int}` is NOT a subtype of `Box{Real}`.",
           "`Box{Int64}`, `Box{Float64}`, `true` — because `Int64 <: Real`.",
           "`Box{Any}`, `Box{Any}`, `true` — all `Box` types collapse to `Box{Any}`.",
           "Error — parametric structs require explicit type parameters."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

### Scoping, Closures and Modules

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION = 0
nothing #| hide
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
x = 10

function f()
    x = 20
    println(x)
end

f()
println(x)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`20` then `10` — `x` inside `f` is a local variable; the global `x` is unchanged.",
           "`20` then `20` — `f` modifies the global `x`.",
           "`10` then `10` — `f` reads the global `x` and prints it.",
           "Error — you cannot shadow a global variable inside a function."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function make_adder(n)
    return x -> x + n
end

add5 = make_adder(5)
println(add5(3))
println(add5(10))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`8` then `15` — `add5` is a closure that captures `n = 5`.",
           "`3` then `10` — `n` is not captured; the closure returns the input unchanged.",
           "`5` then `5` — the closure always returns `n`.",
           "Error — closures cannot capture variables from the enclosing scope."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
function make_counter()
    count = 0
    increment! = () -> (count += 1; count)
    return increment!
end

counter = make_counter()
println(counter())
println(counter())
println(counter())
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`1` then `2` then `3` — the closure mutates the captured variable `count`.",
           "`1` then `1` then `1` — each call creates a fresh `count = 0`.",
           "`0` then `1` then `2` — the count starts at 0 and increments after printing.",
           "Error — closures cannot modify captured variables."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What does the `export` keyword do inside a module?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-50px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["It makes the listed names available without module-prefix qualification when using `using`.",
           "It copies the module's functions into the global namespace permanently.",
           "It restricts access to the listed names from outside the module.",
           "It exports the module to a separate `.jl` file."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
module MyMath
    export square
    square(x) = x^2
    cube(x) = x^3
end

using .MyMath
println(square(4))
println(MyMath.cube(3))
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`16` then `27` — `square` is exported so it can be used directly; `cube` requires the module prefix.",
           "`16` then error — `cube` is not accessible because it wasn't exported.",
           "`16` then `27` — all module functions are accessible without a prefix.",
           "Error — `using .MyMath` only works if `MyMath` is in a separate file."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
NUMQUESTION += 1 
md"**Question $(string(NUMQUESTION)). What is the output of the following code?**"
```

<p style="margin-top:-30px"> </p>

```julia
x = 1

for i in 1:3
    x = x + i
end

println(x)
```

<p style="margin-top:-20px"> </p>

```{julia}
#| echo: false
choices = ["`7` — `for` loops in the REPL/global scope can read and modify outer variables.",
           "`1` — the loop variable `x` is local and does not affect the outer `x`.",
           "Error — `x` is not defined inside the `for` loop scope.",
           "`6` — only the loop body sum `1+2+3` is computed."]
answer = 1
radioq(choices, answer)
```
